questions.title: Swap with Pointers
questions.description: Drag the correct tokens to complete a swap function using pointers:
questions.explanation: Use a temporary variable to store *a, assign *a = *b, and then assign *b = tmp.
hints: Use a temp variable to avoid losing a value

questions.title: Second Element
questions.description: Drag the correct token to return the second element:
questions.explanation: arr + 1 points to the second element, and dereferencing yields its value.
hints: arr + 1 points to the second element

questions.title: Allocate Int Array
questions.description: Drag the correct tokens to allocate an int array:
questions.explanation: Use malloc with n * sizeof(int), and return NULL if allocation fails.

questions.title: Pointer Advance
questions.description: Drag the correct tokens to advance a pointer by one element:
questions.explanation: Dereference p to access the pointer it points to, then add 1 to move to the next element.
hints: Use *p to access the pointer value

questions.title: Free and Null
questions.description: Drag the correct tokens to free memory safely:
questions.explanation: Call free on the pointer and set it to NULL to avoid dangling use.

questions.title: calloc Allocation
questions.description: Drag the correct tokens to allocate with calloc:
questions.explanation: calloc takes count and size and zero-initializes memory.

questions.title: Pointer Parameter
questions.description: Drag the correct token to declare a pointer parameter:
questions.explanation: Use int * for a pointer parameter.

questions.title: Complete Pointer Swap Function
questions.description: Drag tokens to complete a function that swaps two integers using pointers:
questions.explanation: To swap values through pointers: parameters are pointers (int *a), dereference to access values (*a), store in temp, swap dereferenced values.
hints: Parameters must be pointers to modify caller's variables
Use * to dereference and access the actual values

questions.title: Complete Memory Allocation Function
questions.description: Drag tokens to complete safe memory allocation:
questions.explanation: Safe malloc: allocate size * sizeof(type) bytes, check if returned NULL (allocation failed), return NULL on failure.

questions.title: Pointer Declaration and Dereferencing
questions.description: Fill in the blanks about pointers:

questions.text: The & operator is used to get the ___ of a variable
The * operator when used before a pointer ___ the pointer
ptr stores the ___ of x, not its ___

questions.explanation: The & operator gets the memory address of a variable. The * operator dereferences a pointer (accesses the value at that address). Pointers store addresses, not values.
hints: * is used for dereferencing (getting the value)

questions.title: Dynamic Allocation
questions.description: Fill in the blanks about malloc:

questions.text: malloc returns a ___
The total bytes allocated is ___
The memory should be released with ___

questions.explanation: malloc returns a void pointer to allocated memory. The size is calculated in bytes, and the memory must be released with free.
hints: Use free to release allocated memory

questions.title: free Basics
questions.description: Fill in the blanks about free:

questions.text: The function used to release memory is ___
After freeing, p points to ___

questions.explanation: free releases heap memory. After freeing, the pointer becomes invalid until reassigned.
hints: Freed pointers must not be dereferenced
The memory is invalid after free

questions.title: NULL Check
questions.description: Fill in the blanks about NULL pointers:

questions.text: NULL represents a ___ pointer
Dereferencing NULL causes a ___

questions.explanation: NULL is a special pointer value. Dereferencing it is undefined behavior and typically crashes.

questions.title: Pointer Arithmetic
questions.description: Fill in the blanks about pointer arithmetic:

questions.text: p + 2 points to arr[___]
The value printed is ___

questions.explanation: Pointer arithmetic advances by elements. p + 2 points to the third element, which is 3.

questions.title: malloc Check
questions.description: Fill in the blanks about malloc failure:

questions.text: malloc returns ___ on failure
The pointer should be released with ___

questions.explanation: malloc can return NULL. Always free successful allocations.

questions.title: Hukommelsessegmenter (Danish)
questions.description: Udfyld felterne om hukommelsessegmenter i C:

questions.text: Globale variabler gemmes i ___
Lokale variabler gemmes på ___
Dynamisk allokeret hukommelse er i ___

questions.explanation: C-programmer bruger forskellige hukommelsessegmenter: stak (lokale variabler), heap (malloc), data (initialiserede globale), BSS (uinitialiserede globale), tekst (kode).
hints: BSS står for Block Started by Symbol

questions.title: Pointer Terminologi (Danish)
questions.description: Udfyld felterne om pointer operationer:

questions.text: ___ operatoren får adressen, ___ operatoren får værdien
At få værdien på en pointer-adresse kaldes ___
En pointer gemmer en ___ ikke en ___

questions.explanation: Pointer operationer: & får adresse, * derefererer (får værdi). Pointere gemmer adresser, ikke værdier. Dereferering betyder at få værdien på en adresse.

questions.title: Almindelige Hukommelsesfejl (Danish)
questions.description: Udfyld felterne om hukommelsesfejl:

questions.text: Et ___ opstår når allokeret hukommelse aldrig frigives
En ___ peger på hukommelse der er blevet frigivet
At skrive ud over et arrays grænser forårsager ___
At kalde free() to gange på samme pointer er en ___

questions.explanation: Hukommelsesfejl: leaks (ingen free), dinglende pointere (frigivet men stadig brugt), buffer overflow (skriv ud over grænser), double free (frigør to gange).

questions.title: Memory Segments
questions.description: Fill in the blanks about memory segments in C:

questions.text: Global variables are stored in the ___
Local variables are stored on the ___
Dynamically allocated memory is in the ___
Uninitialized global variables go in the ___ segment

questions.explanation: C programs use different memory segments: stack (local vars), heap (malloc), data (initialized globals), BSS (uninitialized globals), text (code).
hints: BSS stands for Block Started by Symbol

questions.title: Pointer Terminology
questions.description: Fill in the blanks about pointer operations:

questions.text: The ___ operator gets the address, the ___ operator gets the value
Accessing the value at a pointer address is called ___
A pointer stores a ___ not a ___

questions.explanation: Pointer operations: & gets address, * dereferences (gets value). Pointers store addresses, not values. Dereferencing means accessing the value at an address.

questions.title: Common Memory Errors
questions.description: Fill in the blanks about memory errors:

questions.text: A ___ occurs when allocated memory is never freed
A ___ points to memory that has been freed
Writing beyond an array's bounds causes ___
Calling free() twice on the same pointer is a ___

questions.explanation: Memory errors: leaks (no free), dangling pointers (freed but still used), buffer overflow (write beyond bounds), double free (free twice), use after free.

questions.title: Basic Bitwise AND
questions.description: What does this code print?
questions.explanation: 12 in binary is 1100, 10 in binary is 1010. Bitwise AND: 1100 & 1010 = 1000 = 8.
hints: Convert both numbers to binary first
AND returns 1 only when both bits are 1

questions.title: Clearing a Specific Bit
questions.description: What does this code print?
questions.explanation: To clear bit n, use x & ~(1 << n). Here: 15 (1111) & ~4 (1011) = 11 (1011). Bit 2 is now 0.
hints: AND with inverted mask clears specific bits
~(1 << 2) creates a mask with all bits 1 except bit 2

questions.title: Checking if a Bit is Set
questions.description: What does this code print?
questions.explanation: To check bit n, use (x & (1 << n)) != 0. Here: 12 (1100) & 8 (1000) = 8, which is != 0, so result is 1 (true).
hints: AND with a mask isolates the specific bit

questions.title: Counting Set Bits Technique
questions.description: What does this code print?
questions.explanation: x & (x-1) clears the rightmost set bit. For 7 (0111): iteration 1: 0111 & 0110 = 0110, iteration 2: 0110 & 0101 = 0100, iteration 3: 0100 & 0011 = 0000. Three set bits.
hints: x & (x-1) is a trick to remove the rightmost 1 bit
Count how many times you can remove a bit

questions.title: Power of Two Check
questions.description: What does this code print?
questions.explanation: Powers of 2 have exactly one set bit. x & (x-1) clears that bit, resulting in 0. 16 (10000) & 15 (01111) = 0, so it's a power of 2.
hints: Powers of 2 have only one bit set
x & (x-1) removes the rightmost set bit
If only one bit exists, the result is 0

questions.title: Find the Memory Leak
questions.description: This code has a memory leak. Which line allocates memory that is never freed?
questions.explanation: Line 5 allocates memory with malloc(100), but there is no corresponding free(str) before the program exits. This causes a memory leak. The fix is to add 'free(str);' before 'return 0;'.
hints: Look for malloc() calls in the code
Check if every malloc has a matching free()
The memory is allocated on line 5 with malloc

questions.title: Use After Free
questions.description: This code contains a use-after-free error. Which line uses memory after it has been freed?
questions.explanation: Line 9 attempts to print the contents of 'ptr' after it has been freed on line 8. This is undefined behavior known as 'use after free'. After freeing memory, you should not access it.
hints: Look for where memory is freed
Check what happens after the free() call
The printf on line 9 uses the pointer after it's freed

questions.title: Double Free Error
questions.description: This code has a double free error. What causes the problem?
options: q and p point to different memory
q and p point to the SAME memory, freed twice
questions.explanation: Line 6 makes q point to the same memory as p. When we free(p) and then free(q), we're freeing the same memory twice, causing a double free error. The fix is to remove one of the free() calls or set the pointer to NULL after freeing.
hints: Look at line 6 - what does q = p do?
Both p and q point to the same memory
Lines 7 and 8 both free the same memory

questions.title: Memory Allocation Size
questions.description: What will this program print? (Assume sizeof(int) = 4 bytes)
questions.explanation: sizeof(int) is typically 4 bytes. 5 * 4 = 20 bytes. The program allocates 20 bytes and prints this value.
hints: What is sizeof(int) on most systems?

questions.title: Memory Leak in Loop
questions.description: How many memory allocations are leaked in this code?
questions.explanation: The loop allocates memory 10 times, but each new malloc() overwrites the previous pointer value. Only the last allocation is freed on line 10. This means 9 out of 10 allocations are leaked. The fix is to free(ptr) at the end of each iteration inside the loop.
hints: How many times does the loop run?
How many times is malloc called?
Each malloc overwrites the previous pointer, losing access to that memory

questions.title: Return Pointer to Local Variable
questions.description: What is wrong with this code?
options: Nothing wrong - it will work fine
Returning pointer to local variable (undefined behavior)
questions.explanation: The function returns a pointer to the local array 'name', which goes out of scope when the function returns. This is undefined behavior. The fix is to either allocate memory with malloc() or use static storage.
hints: What happens to local variables when a function returns?
The array 'name' is allocated on the stack
Returning a pointer to stack memory is dangerous

questions.title: Correct malloc Usage
questions.description: Which is the CORRECT way to allocate memory for a string of 20 characters?
questions.explanation: For a string of 20 characters, you need 21 bytes: 20 for the characters plus 1 for the null terminator '\0'. Always allocate strlen + 1 bytes for strings. Option D is also valid for stack allocation.

questions.title: What does malloc return on failure?
questions.description: What does malloc() return if it fails to allocate memory?
questions.explanation: When malloc() fails to allocate the requested memory (e.g., due to insufficient memory), it returns NULL. It's important to check for NULL before using the pointer to avoid dereferencing a null pointer.
hints: It returns a special pointer value

questions.title: Missing NULL Check
questions.description: What could go wrong with this code?
options: malloc might fail and return NULL, causing a crash on line 6
The array is too large for printf
questions.explanation: With such a large allocation, malloc will likely fail and return NULL. Line 6 then dereferences NULL, causing a segmentation fault. The fix is to add 'if (arr == NULL) { /* handle error */ }' after malloc.
hints: malloc can fail if there's not enough memory
What does malloc return on failure?
Line 6 uses arr without checking if allocation succeeded

questions.title: Memory Leak in Conditional
questions.description: When does this code leak memory?
options: Never - all paths free the memory
questions.explanation: When value < 0, the function returns early on line 7 without freeing the buffer allocated on line 5. This causes a memory leak in the error path. The fix is to add 'free(buffer);' before line 7's return statement.
hints: Look at the early return on line 7
What happens to buffer if we return early?
free(buffer) is only called in the normal path, not the error path

questions.title: Double Free
questions.description: What is the problem with this code?
questions.explanation: Calling free twice on the same pointer is undefined behavior. It can crash or corrupt memory. Set the pointer to NULL after freeing if you need to guard against this.
hints: free should be called exactly once per allocation
Second free uses an already-freed pointer

questions.title: Use After Free
questions.description: What is wrong with this program?
questions.explanation: After free, p points to invalid memory. Dereferencing it is undefined behavior. Access memory only while it is allocated.
hints: Undefined behavior may print garbage or crash

questions.title: Freeing NULL
questions.description: What does this program do?
questions.explanation: Calling free on NULL is a no-op in C. It is safe and does nothing.

questions.title: Wrong sizeof
questions.description: What is the bug in this allocation?
options: Allocates too much memory on 64-bit systems
questions.explanation: sizeof(arr) is the size of the pointer, not the size of int. On 64-bit systems it allocates 10 * 8 bytes, which is larger than needed. The correct expression is sizeof(*arr) or sizeof(int).

questions.title: realloc Failure
questions.description: What is the problem with this code?
questions.explanation: If realloc fails, it returns NULL but the original pointer is lost because it was overwritten. That leaks the original allocation. Use a temporary pointer for realloc.
hints: Assigning directly can lose the original pointer
Use a temp variable for realloc

questions.title: Calloc vs Malloc
questions.description: What's the difference between malloc and calloc?
questions.code_template:
int *arr1 = malloc(10 * sizeof(int));
int *arr2 = calloc(10, sizeof(int));
questions.explanation: calloc initializes allocated memory to zero, malloc doesn't. calloc(n, size) allocates n*size bytes.

questions.title: Realloc Behavior
questions.description: What does realloc return if it cannot resize?
questions.code_template:
int *ptr = malloc(100);
int *new_ptr = realloc(ptr, 10000000000);  // Huge size
questions.explanation: If realloc fails, it returns NULL and the original pointer remains valid. Must check return value!
hints: Original pointer still valid on failure

questions.title: Free NULL
questions.description: What happens when you call free(NULL)?
questions.explanation: free(NULL) is explicitly safe in C. It does nothing. No need to check before freeing.

questions.title: Memory Leak Detection
questions.description: How many bytes are leaked?
questions.explanation: First malloc(100) is leaked when p is reassigned. Second malloc(200) is freed. 100 bytes leaked.

questions.title: Stack vs Heap
questions.description: Which statement is TRUE?
questions.explanation: Stack allocation is faster (just pointer adjustment). Stack auto-frees, heap needs manual free. Both are limited.
hints: Stack allocation is just pointer move

questions.title: calloc Initialization
questions.description: What does calloc do differently from malloc?
questions.code_template: int *arr = calloc(5, sizeof(int));
questions.explanation: calloc allocates memory and sets it to zero. malloc does not initialize memory.

questions.title: Basic malloc Usage
questions.description: What is the correct way to allocate memory for an array of 10 integers?
options: Both B and C are correct
questions.explanation: Both 'malloc(10 * sizeof(int))' and 'malloc(sizeof(int[10]))' correctly allocate memory for 10 integers. The first explicitly multiplies the count by the size of int, while the second uses sizeof on an array type. Both produce the same result and are commonly used patterns.
hints: malloc() takes the number of bytes as an argument
You need to multiply the number of elements by the size of each element
sizeof(int[10]) returns the total size of an array of 10 integers

questions.title: Free After malloc
questions.description: Which call correctly frees the memory?
questions.code_template:
int *p = malloc(sizeof(int));
// ... use p ...
questions.explanation: You free the pointer returned by malloc using free(p).

questions.title: Memory Leak Detection
questions.description: Which of the following code snippets will cause a memory leak?
options: The first allocation of 100 integers is leaked
The second allocation of 50 integers is leaked
questions.explanation: The first allocation of 100 integers is leaked. When ptr is reassigned to point to the second allocation, the reference to the first allocated block is lost without being freed. The second allocation is properly freed before the program ends, but the first allocation remains in memory with no way to free it.
hints: What happens to the first pointer when ptr is reassigned?
Can you free memory if you've lost the pointer to it?
Only one free() call is made, but two allocations occurred

questions.title: realloc with Temp
questions.description: Which is the safest way to use realloc?
questions.code_template:
int *p = malloc(4 * sizeof(int));
// grow to 8 ints
options: int *tmp = realloc(p, 8 * sizeof(int)); if (tmp) p = tmp;
questions.explanation: Using a temp pointer prevents losing the original allocation if realloc fails.

questions.title: calloc vs malloc
questions.description: What is the key difference between calloc and malloc?
options: calloc allocates memory on the stack, malloc on the heap
calloc initializes memory to zero, malloc leaves it uninitialized
calloc cannot be freed with free()
questions.explanation: calloc initializes all allocated memory to zero, while malloc leaves the memory uninitialized with garbage values. calloc also takes two parameters (number of elements and size of each element) whereas malloc takes one parameter (total bytes). Both allocate on the heap and both should be freed with free().
hints: Think about the initial values in the allocated memory
One function sets memory to zero, the other doesn't

questions.title: sizeof Pointer Pitfall
questions.description: What is wrong with this allocation?
questions.code_template: int *p = malloc(10 * sizeof(p));
options: Allocates based on pointer size, not int size
malloc cannot be used for arrays
questions.explanation: sizeof(p) is the size of the pointer, not the size of int. Use sizeof(*p).

questions.title: Dangling Pointer After Free
questions.description: What is the likely outcome of this code?
options: Undefined behavior - may crash or print garbage
questions.explanation: This is undefined behavior due to use-after-free. After free(ptr), the memory is returned to the heap but ptr still points to that address (dangling pointer). Dereferencing it may print 42 if the memory hasn't been overwritten, print garbage if it has, or cause a segmentation fault. The behavior is unpredictable and compiler/system dependent.
hints: What happens to a pointer after the memory it points to is freed?
The pointer value doesn't change, but the memory is no longer yours

questions.title: Free in Loop
questions.description: What is the bug in this code?
questions.explanation: free should be called once. Freeing repeatedly causes undefined behavior.

questions.title: Stack vs Heap Allocation
questions.description: Which variable is allocated on the heap?
options: The memory pointed to by c
questions.explanation: The memory allocated by malloc and pointed to by c is on the heap. Variable a and array b are local variables on the stack. The pointer c itself is also on the stack (it's a local variable), but the memory it points to is on the heap. Static variable d is in the data segment, not on the stack or heap.
hints: malloc always allocates on the heap
Local variables go on the stack
The pointer is different from the memory it points to

questions.title: malloc Return Check
questions.description: Why check the result of malloc?
questions.code_template: int *p = malloc(1000000 * sizeof(int));
options: malloc can return NULL on failure
questions.explanation: malloc can fail and return NULL; you must check before use.

questions.title: realloc Behavior
questions.description: What does realloc do if the new size is larger than the old size?
options: All data is lost and the new memory is uninitialized
The old data is preserved, new space is uninitialized
The old data is preserved, new space is zeroed
questions.explanation: realloc preserves the contents of the original memory up to the minimum of old and new sizes. When expanding, the existing data (ptr[0] to ptr[9]) is preserved, but the newly allocated space (ptr[10] to ptr[19]) is uninitialized and contains garbage values. If you need zeroed memory, you must explicitly set it.
hints: realloc tries to preserve existing data
Only the new portion needs consideration
realloc doesn't initialize new memory like calloc does

questions.title: Free After Return
questions.description: What happens here?
questions.explanation: The heap allocation is returned and freed in main. This is correct.

questions.title: Double Free Error
questions.description: What happens when you call free twice on the same pointer?
options: Nothing - the second free is safely ignored
Undefined behavior - may crash or corrupt heap
questions.explanation: Double free is undefined behavior and a serious error. It can corrupt the heap data structures maintained by the memory allocator, potentially leading to crashes, security vulnerabilities, or subtle bugs. Modern systems may detect this and crash immediately, but the behavior is not guaranteed. Always set pointers to NULL after freeing to help avoid this.
hints: The heap maintains internal data structures
Freeing already-freed memory corrupts these structures
This is a common security vulnerability

questions.title: Lost Pointer
questions.description: What is the issue?
questions.explanation: Incrementing p loses the original pointer. free must be called on the original address.

questions.title: NULL Pointer Check
questions.description: Why should you check if malloc returns NULL?
options: malloc never returns NULL, so the check is unnecessary
malloc returns NULL if memory allocation fails
malloc returns NULL for zero-sized allocations only
questions.explanation: malloc returns NULL if it cannot allocate the requested memory (e.g., not enough memory available, requested size too large). Dereferencing a NULL pointer causes undefined behavior, typically a segmentation fault. Always check malloc's return value before using the pointer to ensure the allocation succeeded.
hints: What if the system runs out of memory?
malloc can fail under certain conditions
Using a NULL pointer will crash your program

questions.title: malloc 0 Bytes
questions.description: What is true about malloc(0)?
questions.code_template: int *p = malloc(0);
options: Always returns a unique pointer you must free
May return NULL or a unique pointer; behavior is implementation-defined
questions.explanation: malloc(0) may return NULL or a unique pointer that can be passed to free.
hints: free is safe on returned pointer

questions.title: Buffer Overflow Detection
questions.description: What is wrong with this code?
options: Buffer overflow - string is longer than allocated space
strcpy cannot be used with malloc'd memory
questions.explanation: This is a buffer overflow. The string "Hello, World!" is 13 characters plus 1 null terminator (14 bytes total), but only 10 bytes were allocated. strcpy will write beyond the allocated buffer, corrupting adjacent heap memory. This can cause crashes, security vulnerabilities, or subtle bugs. Use strncpy or ensure sufficient buffer size.
hints: Count the characters in "Hello, World!" including the null terminator
Writing beyond allocated memory is dangerous

questions.title: Array Allocation
questions.description: Which allocates space for 10 ints?
questions.explanation: Use number of elements times sizeof(int).

questions.title: Memory Alignment
questions.description: On a typical 64-bit system, what is the likely size of this struct in memory?
questions.explanation: Due to memory alignment requirements, the struct is likely 12 bytes. After char a (1 byte), 3 bytes of padding are added to align int b on a 4-byte boundary. After int b (4 bytes), char c takes 1 byte, followed by 3 bytes of trailing padding to ensure the struct size is a multiple of its largest member's alignment (4 bytes). Layout: [a][pad][pad][pad][b][b][b][b][c][pad][pad][pad].
hints: The struct size must be a multiple of its alignment

questions.title: Free NULL
questions.description: What does free(NULL) do?
questions.code_template:
int *p = NULL;
free(p);
questions.explanation: free(NULL) is a no-op and safe.

questions.title: realloc Edge Case
questions.description: What is the correct way to use realloc?
options: int *temp = realloc(ptr, 20 * sizeof(int)); if(temp) ptr = temp;
questions.explanation: Option B is safest. If realloc fails, it returns NULL but the original pointer remains valid. If you directly assign to ptr (option A or D), you lose the original pointer and cannot free the memory, causing a leak. Using a temporary pointer lets you handle failure while preserving the original pointer. Option C works but is inefficient as it doesn't preserve data.
hints: What happens to the original pointer if realloc fails?
You don't want to lose access to your memory

questions.title: Heap Lifetime
questions.description: Which statement is TRUE?
options: Heap allocations are freed automatically at function exit
Heap memory cannot be shared between functions
Heap memory is faster than stack memory
questions.explanation: Heap allocations remain valid until you free them.
hints: Lifetime is not tied to scope

questions.title: Free NULL Pointer
questions.description: What happens when you call free(NULL)?
options: Nothing - it's safe and does nothing
questions.explanation: According to the C standard, calling free(NULL) is explicitly safe and does nothing. This is a convenient feature that simplifies cleanup code - you don't need to check if a pointer is NULL before freeing it. This is why setting pointers to NULL after freeing is a good practice: it makes double-free safe.
hints: The C standard defines this behavior
This is intentionally designed to be safe
It's a convenience feature for cleanup code

questions.title: Free After realloc Fail
questions.description: What is wrong here?
options: free(p) is wrong because p is invalid
questions.explanation: This is correct: if realloc fails, original p is still valid and should be freed.

questions.title: Memory Fragmentation
questions.description: Which allocation pattern is most likely to cause external heap fragmentation?
options: Allocating many small blocks of the same size
Allocating blocks of varying sizes in random order, freeing them in different order
Allocating blocks and freeing them in FIFO order
questions.explanation: External fragmentation occurs when free memory is scattered in small, non-contiguous blocks. Allocating varying sizes in random order and freeing in different order creates the most fragmentation. This leaves the heap with many small holes that may be too small for future allocations, even though total free memory might be sufficient. Same-sized allocations or FIFO freeing tend to reduce fragmentation.
hints: Fragmentation means free space is scattered
Random sizes and order create irregular patterns
Think about what creates many small gaps

questions.title: Uninitialized Memory
questions.description: What is true about malloc'ed memory?
questions.code_template:
int *p = malloc(sizeof(int));
printf("%d\n", *p);
questions.explanation: malloc does not initialize memory, so *p is indeterminate.

questions.title: Use After Free Exploit
questions.description: Why is use-after-free considered a security vulnerability?
options: Freed memory might be reallocated, allowing data corruption
It only affects performance, not security
The compiler prevents this at compile time
questions.explanation: Use-after-free is a serious security vulnerability. After free(), the memory may be reallocated for a different purpose. Writing to the freed pointer can corrupt data structures, potentially allowing attackers to manipulate program behavior, bypass security checks, or execute arbitrary code. The memory might contain sensitive data or function pointers that can be exploited.
hints: Freed memory can be reused by other allocations
Think about what happens if that memory holds important data
Attackers can control what gets allocated in that space

questions.title: calloc Arguments
questions.description: Which arguments does calloc take?
questions.code_template: int *p = calloc(5, sizeof(int));
questions.explanation: calloc takes the number of elements and size of each element.

questions.title: Stack Overflow vs Heap
questions.description: Which is more likely to cause a stack overflow?
options: Deep recursion with large local arrays
questions.explanation: Stack overflow occurs when the stack runs out of space. Deep recursion with large local arrays (option B) consumes stack space rapidly because each function call allocates its local variables on the stack. The stack is typically much smaller than the heap (often 1-8 MB), so deep recursion can exhaust it. malloc uses the heap, which is much larger, and globals are in a separate data segment.
hints: Each recursive call adds to the stack
Local variables are on the stack

questions.title: Free After Pointer Copy
questions.description: What is true about this code?
options: Correct: free can be called through q
questions.explanation: q points to the same allocation as p. Freeing q is valid, but p becomes dangling.

questions.title: Struct Padding Optimization
questions.description: How can you minimize the size of this struct through reordering?
options: struct { char a; char c; int d; double b; }
struct { double b; int d; char a; char c; }
struct { int d; double b; char a; char c; }
questions.explanation: Option B minimizes padding by placing members in descending order of alignment requirements: double (8-byte aligned), int (4-byte aligned), then chars (1-byte aligned). This gives: [double:8][int:4][char][char][pad:2] = 16 bytes. Original order would be: [char][pad:7][double:8][char][pad:3][int:4][pad:4] = 32 bytes. Grouping same-sized types and ordering by size reduces padding.
hints: Larger types usually have stricter alignment requirements
Start with the largest alignment requirement

questions.title: Memory Leak in Loop
questions.description: What is the problem?
questions.explanation: Each allocation is lost at the end of the loop iteration without being freed.

questions.title: Memory Leak in Loop
questions.description: How much memory is leaked in this code?
questions.explanation: 99 allocations are leaked (99 KB). Each iteration allocates 1024 bytes and assigns the pointer to ptr, losing the reference to the previous allocation. After 100 iterations, ptr points only to the last allocation, which is freed. The first 99 allocations are lost and cannot be freed. To fix this, either free ptr in each iteration before reallocating, or use an array to track all allocations.
hints: Each malloc call creates a new allocation
What happens to previous allocations when ptr is reassigned?
Only the last allocation can be freed

questions.title: Heap Allocation Size
questions.description: What does this allocate?
questions.code_template: char *p = malloc(8);
questions.explanation: malloc allocates bytes. This allocates 8 bytes.

questions.title: Returning Stack Memory
questions.description: What is wrong with this function?
options: Returns a pointer to stack memory that is destroyed after function returns
Array cannot be returned from functions
Memory leak - arr is not freed
questions.explanation: This returns a dangling pointer to stack memory. The local array 'arr' is allocated on the stack and is destroyed when create_array() returns. The pointer points to memory that is no longer valid and may be overwritten by subsequent function calls. This is undefined behavior. Solution: allocate with malloc in the function, or pass a buffer from the caller.
hints: Where is the local array allocated?
What happens to stack memory when a function returns?
The lifetime of local variables is limited to the function

questions.title: realloc Shrink
questions.description: What does this do?
questions.code_template:
int *p = malloc(10 * sizeof(int));
p = realloc(p, 5 * sizeof(int));
questions.explanation: realloc can resize to a smaller block, possibly returning the same pointer.

questions.title: sizeof Array vs Pointer
questions.description: What do these sizeof operations return on a 64-bit system?
questions.explanation: sizeof(arr) returns 40 bytes (10 integers * 4 bytes each) because arr is a true array and sizeof returns the total array size. sizeof(ptr) returns 8 bytes (the size of a pointer on a 64-bit system), not the size of the allocated memory. This is a common mistake - sizeof cannot determine the size of dynamically allocated memory.
hints: sizeof on an array gives the total size
sizeof on a pointer gives the pointer size

questions.title: Free After Use
questions.description: What is the issue?
questions.explanation: After free, p points to invalid memory. Dereferencing it is undefined behavior.

questions.title: Memory Allocation Failure
questions.description: What is the best practice when malloc fails?
options: Return NULL and let the caller handle it
Print an error and exit the program immediately
Retry the allocation in a loop until it succeeds
Return a pointer to a static array instead
questions.explanation: Returning NULL and letting the caller handle the error is the best practice. This follows the principle of separation of concerns - the allocation function reports failure, and the caller decides how to handle it (retry, use alternative, exit gracefully, etc.). Exiting immediately (B) doesn't allow cleanup or recovery. Infinite retry (C) can hang the program. Static array (D) has different lifetime semantics.
hints: Who should decide what to do on failure?
Different callers might want to handle errors differently
Library functions typically return error codes/NULL

questions.title: Alignment Requirements
questions.description: Why do memory alignment requirements exist?
options: To waste memory and make structures larger
Some CPUs cannot access misaligned data or it's much slower
It's only a compiler convention with no hardware basis
questions.explanation: Memory alignment is a hardware requirement. Many CPU architectures cannot access misaligned data (e.g., reading a 4-byte int from an odd address) and will fault. Others can access misaligned data but with significant performance penalties, often requiring multiple memory accesses. Alignment ensures data is at addresses that match the CPU's natural word boundaries for optimal performance.
hints: This is about CPU architecture, not just compiler choice
Think about how CPUs fetch data from memory
Misaligned access can cause crashes on some systems

questions.title: realloc with NULL
questions.description: What does realloc(NULL, size) do?
questions.explanation: realloc(NULL, size) is equivalent to malloc(size). This is defined by the C standard and allows realloc to be used uniformly without special-casing the first allocation. Similarly, realloc(ptr, 0) is equivalent to free(ptr) on many systems (though this is implementation-defined). This makes realloc flexible for dynamic array growth.
hints: The C standard defines this behavior
Think about what makes sense for a NULL pointer
This allows realloc to handle the first allocation

questions.title: Memory Initialization
questions.description: Which statements about memory initialization are correct?
options: Only b is initialized to zero
b and d are initialized to zero
a, b, c, and d are all initialized to zero
questions.explanation: calloc initializes memory to zero, so b is zero. Static and global variables are initialized to zero by the C standard, so d is zero. malloc leaves memory uninitialized (a has garbage). Automatic local variables like c are uninitialized (garbage). Understanding initialization is crucial for avoiding bugs from reading uninitialized memory.
hints: Static and global variables have special initialization
Local variables are not automatically initialized

questions.title: Buffer Overflow Prevention
questions.description: Which is the safest way to copy a string to a fixed-size buffer?
questions.explanation: Option C is safest. strncpy(buffer, input, 31) copies at most 31 characters, leaving room for the null terminator which is explicitly added. strncpy doesn't always null-terminate (if input is >= n characters), so explicit termination is needed. strcpy (A) can overflow. Option B might not null-terminate. memcpy (D) doesn't handle strings properly and doesn't stop at null terminators.
hints: You need room for the null terminator
strncpy doesn't always add a null terminator

questions.title: Memory Fragmentation Types
questions.description: What is the difference between internal and external fragmentation?
options: Internal: wasted space within allocated blocks; External: unusable gaps between blocks
Internal: gaps between blocks; External: wasted space within blocks
Internal fragmentation doesn't exist in C
questions.explanation: Internal fragmentation is wasted space within an allocated block (e.g., requesting 7 bytes but getting 16, wasting 9 bytes). External fragmentation is unusable free space scattered in small gaps between allocated blocks. Both reduce memory efficiency. Internal fragmentation is caused by allocation granularity and alignment; external by varying allocation sizes and free patterns.
hints: Both waste memory but in different ways

questions.title: Mark ALL places where free() should be called
questions.description: Marker ALLE de steder i koden nedenfor, hvor free() skal kaldes for at undgå memory leaks:
questions.explanation: Alle tre pointere (p1, p2, og str) skal frigives før programmet slutter. Det korrekte sted er før return-statement (option D). Man skal kalde free(p1), free(p2), og free(str) alle sammen før linje 14. Options A, B, og C er ikke korrekte fordi vi stadig bruger variablerne senere i programmet.
hints: Alle malloc-kald skal have en tilsvarende free
free skal kaldes før programmet slutter
Men vi skal stadig bruge variablerne til printf

questions.title: Mark ALL places where free() should be called
questions.description: Marker ALLE de steder i funktionen nedenfor, hvor free() skal kaldes:
questions.explanation: På linje 12 skal free(data) kaldes før return, fordi data allerede er allokeret men buffer allocation fejlede. Dette er et klassisk memory leak i error-handling paths. Linje 18 og 19 er korrekte for den normale path. Linje 7 er OK fordi data==NULL på det tidspunkt (ingen allocation skete). Option C er forkert fordi buffer ikke blev allokeret successfully.
hints: Hvad er allokeret når hver return bliver nået?
Error handling skal også frigive hukommelse

questions.title: Marker ALLE korrekte udsagn om memory management
questions.description: Vælg ALLE korrekte udsagn om dynamisk hukommelsesallokering i C:
options: realloc() bevarer eksisterende data når størrelsen øges
sizeof(pointer) giver størrelsen af allokeret hukommelse
questions.explanation: B er korrekt: calloc() nulstiller hukommelsen. C er korrekt: free(NULL) er eksplicit tilladt i C-standarden og gør ingenting. D er korrekt: realloc() bevarer data op til minimum af gammel og ny størrelse. A er forkert: malloc() efterlader hukommelsen uinitialiseret. E er forkert: sizeof(pointer) giver størrelsen af selve pointeren (8 bytes på 64-bit systemer), ikke det allokerede område.
hints: sizeof på en pointer giver pointer-størrelsen

questions.title: NULL Pointer Dereference
questions.description: What happens when this code runs?
questions.explanation: Line 5 dereferences a NULL pointer, causing a segmentation fault. Always check if a pointer is NULL before dereferencing. Fix: Add a NULL check before using the pointer, or initialize it to point to valid memory.
hints: Line 5 tries to write to address 0

arr + 2 points to the third element

questions.explanation: x is a local variable. Returning &x returns a pointer to a stack variable that goes out of scope. Dereferencing it is undefined behavior.

questions.title: Pointer to Array Element

questions.title: Pointer Size
questions.description: On a 64-bit system, what is sizeof a pointer?
questions.explanation: All pointers are same size (8 bytes on 64-bit), regardless of what they point to.

questions.title: Pointer Aliasing

questions.explanation: p and q both point to x (aliasing). Changing *p changes x, so *q also sees 20.

questions.title: Pointer to Const vs Const Pointer

questions.explanation: int * const p makes pointer constant (can't change where it points). const int *p makes pointee constant.

questions.title: Const Pointer

p is read-only but *p is not
questions.explanation: A const pointer cannot change its address, but the value it points to can change.

questions.title: Pointer Arithmetic with Different Types

questions.explanation: Pointer subtraction returns the number of elements between two pointers, not bytes. p2 - p1 = 4 - 1 = 3 elements. The result is independent of sizeof(long).

Count the array positions between p1 and p2

questions.title: Pointer to Const

questions.explanation: Pointer to const can change address, but cannot modify the pointed value.

questions.title: Array of Pointers vs Pointer to Array
questions.description: What is the key difference in these declarations?

hints: p1 is an array containing 3 pointers
Count how many pointer-sized objects each holds

questions.title: Void Pointer Basics

questions.title: Double Pointer Update
questions.description: What does this program print?

questions.title: Void Pointer Arithmetic

hints: Can you do arithmetic on a type with no size?
This is a compiler extension, not standard C

questions.explanation: int (*p)[3] is a pointer to an array of 3 ints.

questions.title: Pointer to Const

questions.explanation: const int *p means p points to a const int. You cannot modify the value through p (*p = 15 is invalid), but you can change where p points (p = &y is valid).
hints: const int* means the pointed-to value is const
Can you change what p points to?
Can you change the value through p?

questions.title: Const Pointer
questions.description: Which line causes a compilation error?
questions.explanation: int *const p means p is a const pointer to int. You can modify the value through p (*p = 15 is valid), but you cannot change where p points (p = &y is invalid).
hints: int* const means the pointer itself is const
Can you change the value at the pointed-to location?

questions.title: Swap by Value

questions.title: Const Pointer to Const
questions.description: Which lines cause compilation errors?
options: Both Line A and Line B

hints: The first const protects the value
The second const protects the pointer

questions.title: Pointer to Function
questions.description: Which declaration is a pointer to a function taking int and returning int?
questions.explanation: Parentheses make f a pointer to a function: int (*f)(int).

questions.title: Pointer Comparison
questions.description: What does this code print?
questions.explanation: Pointers to elements of the same array can be compared. p2 points to arr[3] and p1 points to arr[1]. Since arr[3] is at a higher address, p2 > p1 is true (1).
hints: Both pointers point into the same array
p2 is at arr[3], p1 is at arr[1]

questions.title: Pointer Indexing
questions.description: What does this program print?

questions.title: Pointer Comparison Across Objects
questions.description: What is the behavior of this code?
options: Always prints 'p2 is less or equal'
questions.explanation: Comparing pointers to different objects (not part of the same array or object) using relational operators (<, >, <=, >=) is undefined behavior in C. Only equality comparison (==, !=) is well-defined.
hints: x and y are separate objects
Relational comparison requires pointers to the same object/array
Only == and != are well-defined for unrelated pointers

questions.title: Pointer Subtraction Same Array
questions.description: What does this code print?
questions.explanation: Pointer subtraction returns the number of elements between pointers. p2 points to str[4] and p1 points to str[1], so p2 - p1 = 4 - 1 = 3.
hints: Subtraction gives element count, not bytes
p2 is at index 4, p1 is at index 1

questions.title: Pointer Subtraction Different Objects
questions.description: What is the behavior of this code?
questions.explanation: Pointer subtraction is only defined for pointers to elements of the same array (or one past the end). Subtracting pointers to different objects (a and b) is undefined behavior.
hints: a and b are separate objects, not array elements
Pointer arithmetic requires pointers to the same array/object
This violates the rules of pointer subtraction

questions.title: Null Pointer Initialization

hints: The comparison checks if p is null

questions.title: Null Pointer Dereference
questions.description: What happens when this code runs?
questions.explanation: Dereferencing a NULL pointer (*p) is undefined behavior and typically causes a segmentation fault or program crash at runtime.
hints: What happens when you access invalid memory?
This is a runtime error, not compile-time

questions.title: Double Pointer and NULL
questions.description: What does this code print?
questions.explanation: pp points to p, which is NULL. *pp dereferences pp to get the value of p (NULL), so *pp == NULL is true. pp itself is not NULL (it points to p), so pp == NULL is false.
hints: pp points to p, not NULL
p is NULL, but pp is not

p starts at arr[0], then moves to arr[1]

questions.title: Function Pointer Return Type

options: Function taking int, returning pointer to function
Pointer to function taking int, returning int
questions.explanation: func is a function that takes an int and returns a pointer to a function. The returned function takes two ints and returns an int. Read inside-out: func(int x) is a function, (*...) makes it return a pointer, (int, int) are parameters, and int is the final return type.
hints: func(int x) is a function taking int
The * means it returns a pointer to a function

questions.explanation: p + 2 moves 2 ints forward (8 bytes if sizeof(int) = 4). Casting to char* and subtracting gives the byte difference: 2 * 4 = 8 bytes.
